text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

 Dr Emilia Carter 
 Northbrook Medical Centre 

 12 Northbrook Road, London, SE5 2JL 
Dear  Dr Emilia Carter 
 Michael Green , DOB: 14-02-1989, Hospital No:  H728391  NHS No.  9430021178 
 27C Millstream Court, London, SE15 3PA  

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. He continues to attend with a detailed paper seizure calendar, which he completes daily and brings to appointments. Since I last saw him on 18 June 2025 he has maintained careful logs of events, sleep, and medication timings. He reports ongoing fatigue at the end of working days and occasional light-headedness when standing quickly. He denies rashes or new neurological symptoms. 

He is currently taking Levetiracetam 1000 mg b.d. and Lamotrigine 150 mg b.d. without reported missed doses. He notes mild morning nausea that settles with food. There have been no emergency department presentations and no injuries. 

Review of his paper diary from 01 July 2025 to 30 September 2025 shows regular entries without gaps. Across this interval, there have been no witnessed convulsive episodes recorded by him or observers, nor any events he felt were likely to represent clinical seizures. He describes two brief spells of momentary loss of train of thought during periods of stress at work, each lasting a few seconds, without automatisms or post-event confusion; he documented these as “possible lapses – uncertain significance” in the calendar. He has not driven and continues to follow prior safety advice around bathing and heights. 

We discussed sleep hygiene and the value of continuing his paper-based record, which remains clear and consistent. He prefers the calendar to digital applications and finds it easy to update immediately after work. He will continue to bring the log to future visits. 

Plan: no medication changes today given stability in his diary and absence of witnessed events suggesting clinical seizures. Routine bloods (U&Es, LFTs) will be arranged with his GP in the next month for ongoing monitoring. I have asked him to mark any episodes with clear behavioural change, witnessed impairment, or post-event confusion distinctly in the calendar and to contact us sooner if these occur. I will review him again in four months, or earlier if his diary indicates a change. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Across this interval, there have been no witnessed convulsive episodes recorded by him or observers, nor any events he felt were likely to represent clinical seizures.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter reviews the patient's seizure diary from 01 July 2025 to 30 September 2025 (3 months). It explicitly states there were no witnessed convulsive episodes and no events he felt were likely to represent clinical seizures. Two brief questionable lapses were noted but documented as uncertain significance and without features suggestive of clinical seizures. There is no indication of seizures outside this 3-month window since the last review on 18 June 2025, but the letter does not confirm a seizure-free period of at least 6 months. Therefore, he is currently seizure-free for multiple months, not meeting the 6-month criterion for 'seizure free' by strict definition.
seizure_frequency_number: Across this interval, there have been no witnessed convulsive episodes recorded by him or observers, nor any events he felt were likely to represent clinical seizures.
